rationale: The citing paper does more than list the 2006 study as background; it explicitly explains the goal-gradient result and then uses it to delimit its own research domain. Stourm et al. argue that Kivetz et al. concerns purchase acceleration toward retailer-set goals in nonlinear programs, whereas their setting is linear programs without explicit goals and with a different focal decision (when to redeem). This citation is used in conceptual positioning to define boundary conditions and motivate why a different redemption model is needed.
relation_description: The citing paper qualifies the scope of Kivetz et al. (2006) by arguing that its goal-gradient mechanism is tied to explicit redemption goals and purchase effort, not to redemption timing in linear programs. This narrowing of applicability helps justify the citing paper's distinct modeling focus on stockpiling and redemption choice.
